Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: We are going to break down the problem into steps

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: For each case_id we need to get the ordered sequence of activities ordered by timestamp We can use the LIST aggregate function with an ORDER BY clause to get the sequence as a list

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We then group by this sequence to get the count of cases per variant process variant

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We then determine the top K variants by frequency Lets assume K equals 5 for the example but we can make it a parameter

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We then filter the original event_log to only include cases that have their sequence in the top K variants

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note that we cannot directly use the list in a WHERE clause without storing it in a CTE or subquery

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: We can use the list as a key Alternatively we can assign an identifier to each variant the list and then use that

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Create a CTE that for each case_id produces the activity sequence as a list

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then we count the cases per variant and rank them

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then we get the top K variants

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then we get the case_ids that have these variants

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Finally we select all events from the original event_log for these case_ids

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The original requirement is to return all events from the original event log that belong only to cases which are part of the top K variants

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: So we can do SELECT all columns FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM top_cases

Name: Validation - IND
Text: The output might be ordered by case_id and then timestamp The problem does not specify the order of the output We can order by case_id and then timestamp to have a nice view

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - PE
Text: What if two variants have the same count The problem says top K variants by their frequency We are using ROW_NUMBER which will assign a unique rank even if the counts are the same We might want to use RANK or DENSE_RANK if we want to allow ties

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Lets use RANK

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then when we filter by rank less than or equal to 5 we might get more than K variants if there are ties

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: The problem does not specify so we will use RANK to include ties Usually in such contexts we include ties

Name: Validation - PE
Text: In DuckDB we can use the LIST aggregate function to create a list of activities and DuckDB allows grouping by arrays
